title: solLabel / gcLabel click listeners attached in constructor are never removed in dispose()
Stellata.dispose() leaves observeControls' window listeners attached
description:
Stellata.dispose() (src/client/stellata.ts:2911-2940) carefully removes every canvas listener Stellata itself attached, but never calls this.observeControls.disable() — and ObserveControls owns its own pointerdown/pointerup/pointercancel/pointermove/wheel listeners (src/client/observe-controls.ts:105-127, attached on enable()).

If dispose() runs while the user is in OBSERVE mode, four window listeners and one canvas listener survive. Each closes over the ObserveControls instance, which retains the camera + canvas — so the entire teardown the rest of dispose() does is undone for the parts those listeners reach.

Same family of audit miss as the GPU leaks the PR fixed (Stellata.dispose() doesn't release everything it owns). Listeners aren't GPU resources but the audit was implicitly the broader 'walk the dispose chain' the c2l description called for.

Direction: have Stellata.dispose() unconditionally call this.observeControls.disable(). disable() is already idempotent (early-returns when not enabled), so no caller-side mode check is needed.

title: DustField has no dispose() — Data3DTexture (~128 MiB) leaks past Stellata.dispose()
description:
The c2l audit fixed every star-pipeline / cloud / milkyway / galactic resource but missed the largest single GPU allocation in the app: the dust voxel grid.

DustField (src/client/dust-loader.ts:134, texture allocated at line 154 — Data3DTexture, RedFormat/UnsignedByte, 64 chunks × 2 MiB ≈ 128 MiB) has no dispose() at all. The texture is wired into shader uniforms by Stellata.attachDust (src/client/stellata.ts:1124-1142) and shared with the Milky Way layer; nothing releases it when Stellata.dispose() runs (src/client/stellata.ts:2911-2940).

This is a hot variant of exactly the leak class PR #14 enumerated — the audit description listed coreMaskMaterial / particleMesh / clouds / galactic{Disc,Grid} but didn't crawl into the dust loader's owned texture.

Why it matters: today dispose() runs only on page navigation so the leak is academic, but the c2l ticket framed itself as 'walk the dispose chain end-to-end'. Missing the 128 MiB texture is the biggest miss to backfill before any hot-reload or scene-swap path lands.

Direction: add DustField.dispose() that calls this.texture.dispose() (and clears the listener array); call it from Stellata.dispose() — most cleanly via a kept reference to the attached DustField (currently only the renderer holds it through the uniform).

title: Roll-snap fix (stellata-a8w) only covers animated exit; hard-switch + observe bail-out paths still misalign camera.up
description:
PR #23 added a camera.up realignment in finishObserveTransition (stellata.ts:2893) that only runs after the animated observe→navigate lerp.

The same root cause exists at two other observe-exit sites that bypass that animation:

1. **stellata.ts:983-990** (startObserveExit hard-switch branch). When called with animate=false, or when focusedStar===null at exit, controls.target is snapped, controls.update() runs lookAt, and TrackballControls re-enables — all without realigning camera.up. A user who pitches in observe and exits via this path gets the same roll snap a8w fixed elsewhere.
2. **stellata.ts:1029-1037** (setFocus while in observe). When focus changes mid-observe, the code flips cameraMode to navigate and re-enables controls without realigning camera.up. The next controls.update() will roll-snap.

Why it matters: a8w identified the root cause as 'camera.up stale after observe pitch + lookAt re-resolves roll'. The fix is one-line; applying it on only one of three exit paths leaves a regression latent for any non-animated exit.

Suggested direction: extract `alignCameraUpToQuaternion()` and call from all three exit points — finishObserveTransition (already done), the hard-switch branch, and the setFocus-while-observe branch.

title: Unfocusing while close to a star disengages uPinFocusToCenter — focal star jumps on-screen
description:
# Problem

When the user unfocuses while sitting close to a focused star (sub-µpc orbit), the focal star visibly jumps to a different screen position even though no camera motion was intended. Diagnosed via debug.pin() HUD across two screenshots:

**Pre-unfocus (focused, pin engaged):**
- focus: 32 (Rigel), pin: YES, mode: navigate, warp/aim: false
- target.lengthSq() < 1e-12: true
- target = (0, 0, 0)
- camera = (1.85e-6, -2.61e-6, 3.95e-5)
- distCam = 3.96e-5 pc, controls.minDistance = 4.19e-6 pc
- Star is centred via the shader pin path (uPinFocusToCenter).

**Post-unfocus (focus cleared, pin disengaged):**
- focus: null, pin: NO, flips: 2, off-frames: 142
- target.lengthSq() < 1e-12: false
- target = (51.6, 257, -37.7) pc — former focal star's absolute world position
- camera = (51.6, 257, -37.7) (same, plus a sub-µpc residual)
- distCam = 3.96e-5 pc (preserved)
- Rigel renders well off-centre — visible jump on the canvas.

# Root cause

`uPinFocusToCenter` engages only when `controls.target.lengthSq() < 1e-12` — see the per-frame pin guard in stellata.ts:872 and the GLSL bypass in star.vert.glsl. The guard was designed around the invariant that a focused star sits at local (0,0,0) after `recenterOrigin`. On unfocus, `setFocus(null)` calls `recenterOrigin(0,0,0)` to put the floating origin back at Sol so URL-serialised camera poses are in absolute space (intentional — see the comment at stellata.ts:1119). That recentre shifts target by the former focal star's full world-position, breaking the lengthSq invariant. Without the pin, the float32-cancellation drift in the projection chain (the exact problem the pin was created to fix in stellata-a7d.2 / commit 5035edf) becomes visible — the focal star appears to jump.

This is the same class of bug as stellata-a7d.2.1 (warp arrival residual) and stellata-a7d.2.8 (default-load focus offset), but triggered by the unfocus path.

# Secondary observation: HUD ring disappears, arrows remain

Reported simultaneously: when the HUD is on and the user unfocuses from a star in front of them, the focus ring disappears (expected — no focus) but the Sol/GC arrows stay drawn. The arrows are mode-bound (navigate), not focus-bound; the ring is focus-bound. The asymmetry feels odd from a UX standpoint — either both should persist or both should retire on unfocus. Bundled here because it's the same user-visible event (unfocus while close).

# Possible fixes (for design exploration; user has not yet picked)

A. **Don't recentre on unfocus.** Leave worldOffset at the former focal star until the user moves significantly. Pin disengages cleanly (focus=null), but target stays at (0,0,0) so float32 cancellation doesn't drift. URL serialisation needs to encode worldOffset alongside camera (or always recompute from camera_local + worldOffset).

B. **Recentre to camera position on unfocus** instead of (0,0,0). Camera ends up at local (0,0,0); former focal star is at small negative camera offset. Float32 stays clean. URL still encodes camera_world (camera_local + worldOffset) so no semantic change.

C. **Animate the unfocus transition** so the float32 drift is masked by motion (smooth zoom-out). Doesn't fix the underlying invariant break, just hides it.

D. **Extend the pin guard** to fire on unfocused-but-target-near-a-star cases, projecting through that star's local position. Complex, edge cases.

B feels cleanest — it preserves the invariant the pin needs without changing URL semantics. Verify against floating-origin design assumptions before committing.

# Where to look

- src/client/stellata.ts:872 (per-frame pin guard)
- src/client/stellata.ts:1100-1140 (setFocus null branch — recenterOrigin(0,0,0))
- src/client/stellata.ts:1179-1208 (recenterOrigin)
- src/client/shaders/star.vert.glsl (uPinFocusToCenter use)
- src/client/pin-debug-hud.ts (debug.pin() — used to diagnose this; both screenshots from this HUD)
- bd memory `stellata-a7d2-pin-mechanism` for full pin-design context

# Branch

Will land on the same a7d.2 worktree / PR #22 if scoped tight; otherwise a follow-up branch.
notes: Secondary observation (HUD ring vs Sol/GC arrows asymmetry on unfocus) split out to stellata-3c8 — UX decision, not part of the numerical-precision fix.
close_reason: Fixed: setFocus(null) no longer recenters worldOffset to Sol — origin stays at the former focal object so the projection chain remains float32-clean across unfocus (no visible jump). URL serialisation generalised: new optional worldOffset field (Float32 vec3, FIELDS_V2 bit 20, appended for graceful fwd-compat) carries the absolute anchor when no focus is active, so close-orbit unfocus poses round-trip exactly. Anchor concept is type-agnostic — extends naturally to clouds/planets/probes/etc. without coupling to catalog ref space. Verified in browser: pre/post-unfocus screen position is invariant; URL share-and-reopen lands at the same close-orbit pose. Memory stellata-a7d2-pin-mechanism updated. Secondary observation (HUD ring vs Sol/GC arrow asymmetry) split to stellata-3c8.

title: build-catalog.ts crashes on fresh checkout because public/ dir doesn't exist
description: On the first GitHub Actions run of deploy.yml (PR #25 merge → main bumped 1.0.7→1.0.8), npm run build:catalog failed with ENOENT on public/catalog.bin. Cause: public/ is gitignored entirely and build-catalog.ts (lines 806/813/833) writes there without ensuring the directory exists. Local builds only work because public/ already exists from prior runs. Other build scripts (sync-dust.ts, build-clouds.py) already mkdir correctly. Fix: add fs.mkdir(dirname(OUT_BIN), { recursive: true }) before the first writeFile in scripts/build-catalog.ts. This unblocks deploy.yml and also makes the project buildable from a fresh clone.

title: Rigil Kentaurus / Toliman render as a single star (and warp Rigil→Toliman is a no-op)
description:
While testing stellata-dx7 (61 Cyg disc-edge fix) on 2026-05-03, observed that α Cen A (Rigil Kentaurus) and α Cen B (Toliman) appear as a single star in render rather than the expected close pair.

Two distinct symptoms (may share a root cause):

1. Visually only one star is present at the α Cen system, when both A and B should be visible as a tight resolved pair.

2. With Rigil Kentaurus focused, attempting to warp to Toliman is a no-op — nothing happens. This raises the question of whether Toliman is even being rendered (i.e. did it survive ingest at all, or is it dropped during catalog build / cross-match / binary inference).

# Possible interactions

- Could be at least partially confounded with stellata-a7d (physical sizes for binary components). With per-component physical sizing not yet correct, A's disc may simply be visually swallowing B even when both are rendered. But the 'warp does nothing' symptom suggests B is missing from the search/render pipeline entirely, not just visually occluded.

# Next debugging steps

- Verify both HIPs (HIP 71683 = α Cen A, HIP 71681 = α Cen B) are in the binary catalog written by scripts/build-catalog.ts. Run scripts/verify-catalog.ts and grep for both.
- Check that Toliman is in the search index (public/search-index.json) — if not, the typeahead can't find it and warp won't dispatch.
- If Toliman is in the catalog but warp dispatch is silently failing, instrument the warp / search→focus path.
- Coordinate with stellata-a7d — sizing fix may make the visual one-star-vs-two question moot, but won't fix the warp issue if Toliman is genuinely missing.

# Relates

- stellata-a7d (binary component physical sizes)
- stellata-dx7 (the bug fix that surfaced this — disc edge blending; closed)
notes:
# Investigation 2026-05-04 — root cause is identical catalog positions

Both HIPs are present and intact in the build pipeline:

- AT-HYG `data/athyg_33_classic_ids.csv` rows 1451151 (Toliman, HIP 71681, K1V) and 1451193 (Rigil Kentaurus, HIP 71683, G2V) — both have full coords, dist=1.3248 pc.
- Both rows have IDENTICAL x0/y0/z0 = (-0.495, -0.414, -1.157). AT-HYG stores α Cen A and B at the same system barycentric position; real physical separation is ~17.6 AU ≈ 8.5e-5 pc, below catalog precision.
- Both make it through the build into `public/search-index.json` as separate catalog indices: i=289911 (Rigil Kentaurus) and i=305856 (Toliman).

So Toliman is NOT missing — both are rendered, both are searchable.

# Why one star is visible

With identical world positions, both stars project to the same pixel cluster every frame. The brighter Rigil A (V=-0.01) opaque disc draws over the dimmer Toliman B (V=1.35) opaque disc. User sees one star.

# Why the warp is a no-op

`startWarp` at src/client/stellata.ts:1727 has `if (distPc < 1e-6) return;`. With A and B at identical positions, distPc = 0 → early return → no warp dispatched, no error, no UI feedback. From the user's POV: nothing happens.

# Fix paths (scope-graded)

**Smaller, scoped (independent of a7d.2):**
1. Make warp-between-co-located degrade gracefully: still call `setFocus(destIdx)` so the focused star changes even when camera doesn't move. User sees the search row, focus ring, scale bar, etc. switch over to the "other" star.

**Larger (proper physical-reality fix, would need its own issue):**
2. Synthesise per-component offsets at build time for known close binaries (α Cen A/B and ~hundreds of others). Requires a binary-separation data source — Washington Double Star Catalog (WDS) is the canonical catalogue. Build pipeline gains a `data/wds.txt` ingest, plus per-pair logic to nudge component positions perpendicular to line-of-sight by their physical separation. Both stars become visually + warpably distinct.

Path (1) is a 5-line fix and unblocks the immediate UX. Path (2) is a follow-up — possibly under stellata-a7d (which owns binary-component physical sizing).

# Confounds with a7d.2

The visual-merge fix likely lands as part of a7d.2's binary-component physical sizing work, OR through path (2) above. Either way: a7d.2 is in_progress (different session, PR #22), so the rendering side stays out of bounds for this session.

title: Observe-mode HUD direction-finder arrows disappear when target is behind camera
description:
In observe mode with the HUD enabled, the direction-finding arrows for off-axis targets (POIs, Sol, galactic centre, etc.) disappear when the target appears to be behind the camera. This breaks the implicit contract that there should always be a way to navigate back to a tracked target — once the user yaws/pitches the camera so the target falls behind them, the arrow vanishes and they're stranded with no visual cue to point them back.

Navigate mode handles this correctly (off-screen targets keep their direction indicator); observe mode should match.

# Expected

Arrows for tracked targets remain visible at the screen edge regardless of where the target is in the camera's hemisphere — front, side, or fully behind. The arrow should point toward the projected great-circle direction the user needs to rotate to bring the target back into view.

# Investigation entry points

- src/client/poi-overlay.ts — observe-mode pinned-star labels + arrows + rings. Probably the place that gates arrow visibility on a positive z (in front of camera) check that should be removed or replaced with a behind-camera projection.
- src/client/hud-overlay.ts — Sol / GC HUD arrows. Likely the same gating pattern.
- src/client/arrow-path.ts — shared arrow geometry helper. May or may not be the source of the visibility logic.
- src/client/observe-controls.ts — look-around controller (for context on what 'behind camera' means in observe-mode coords).

# Likely fix shape

The standard fix is: when a target's view-space z is positive (behind camera in OpenGL convention), don't try to perspective-project it (the projection sign-flips and the on-screen position lies). Instead, project the direction vector onto the camera's near-plane hemisphere and clamp to the screen edge in the direction the user needs to turn. Navigate mode's existing distance-vector / focus-ring overlays handle this — see distance-vector-overlay.ts for prior art on near-plane clipping (referenced in docs/overlays.md).

# Acceptance

- POI arrows in observe mode remain visible at the screen edge when the POI is behind the camera, pointing toward the rotation needed to recover the target.
- Sol / GC HUD arrows behave the same.
- Forward-of-camera behaviour unchanged (no regression on visible-target arrow placement).
- Mobile + desktop both behave the same way.
notes:
# Implementation 2026-05-04 — PR #27 (worktree-fix-dy2-behind-camera-arrows)

Root cause: `projectToScreen` in both hud-overlay.ts and poi-overlay.ts returns null when target view-space z >= -near (target behind camera). Both direction derivation paths (aux-step and direct target projection) depended on it. When both failed, the existing code hid the arrow — the bug.

Fix: added `viewSpaceScreenDir` to src/client/arrow-path.ts. Transforms the world-space direction into camera-local space (rotation only, no perspective divide) and uses (x, -y) as screen direction. Returns null only when direction is exactly along camera axis. Wired as third-tier fallback after aux-step and target-projection in both overlays.

Forward-of-camera behaviour unchanged because the new fallback only fires when both prior paths return null.

Files:
- src/client/arrow-path.ts (+ helper, + import THREE type)
- src/client/arrow-path.test.ts (NEW, 8 vitest cases)
- src/client/hud-overlay.ts (+ third fallback in updateOne)
- src/client/poi-overlay.ts (+ third fallback in onFrame loop)
- docs/galactic-overlay.md (third path documented)
- package.json (1.0.9 → 1.0.10)

Mechanical gates: typecheck clean, 254/254 vitest, build clean, all CI green on PR #27.

# Browser smoke pending

Math is unit-tested but I (Claude) can't drive a browser interactively. Acceptance items requiring user verification:
- Pin a POI in observe, yaw past 90° — arrow stays at screen edge pointing to required rotation.
- Sol arrow when focused on non-Sol star and yawed away.
- GC arrow when galactic centre falls behind camera.
- Confirm no regression on forward-of-camera arrow placement.

Closes on PR merge.

title: Investigate why Barnard's Star and Sol render similar size at unfocused min distance
description:
User report: at GLOBAL_MIN_DIST_PC (5e-3 pc) viewing Sol vs Barnard's Star side-by-side in NAVIGATE mode (not chart mode), the two stars appear similar in apparent size. Expected ratio is ~5.4× by physical radius (Sol 1.035 R☉ vs Barnard 0.190 R☉ ≈ 1.9 R♃).

# Investigation outcome (2026-05-03)

Catalog data is **correct**. Confirmed via direct binary inspection:

| Star | R (R☉) | absmag | spect/lum |
|---|---|---|---|
| Sol | 1.035 | 4.85 | G/Ib |
| Barnard | 0.190 | 13.23 | M/Iab |
| Proxima | 0.085 | 15.44 | M/Ib |
| Sirius A | 1.816 | 1.45 | A/? |
| Vega | 2.687 | 0.60 | A/Ib |
| Rigel | 74.67 | -6.93 | B/VII |
| Betelgeuse | 697.5 | -5.47 | M/VII |

The radius ratio is faithfully encoded. The discrepancy is in the rendering pipeline.

# Why both stars look the same at d = 5e-3 pc

At ~1000 AU separation, both stars are sub-pixel in physical disc size (even Rigel-class giants are sub-arcsec at that range). The shader does `pxSize = max(appSize, physSize)`, so when physSize is sub-pixel the rendered size is determined entirely by `appSize`.

`appSize` in navigate mode (NOT chart mode) uses a physically-grounded Gaussian-PSF model: perceived radius ∝ √(brightness above threshold). The formula:

```glsl
brightness = clamp((uMaxAppMag - appMag) / sizeSpan, 0, 1);
appSize = mix(uSizeMin, uSizeMax, sqrt(brightness));
```

This √Δm shape models how a true point source appears as the eye integrates intensity above its detection threshold. The shape is correct.

The problem is the **`clamp(0, 1)`**. At d = 5e-3 pc:
- Sol appMag ≈ -16.7 (DM = -21.5 + absmag 4.85)
- Barnard appMag ≈ -8.3 (-21.5 + 13.23)

Both are far brighter than `uMaxAppMag` (≈ 6.5) → `brightness` clamps to 1 for both → both get `uSizeMax`. The ~8-magnitude (≈1500×) brightness difference is invisible because of the saturation, AND the physical radius signal is sub-pixel and discarded by the max().

# Physical fidelity question

The PSF √brightness shape is correct in principle, but the hard saturation breaks the physics at the bright end — a real point source at appMag -16 doesn't 'cap out' at the same perceived size as one at appMag -2. The bloom/diffraction footprint keeps growing. The clamp is probably defensive UX (don't let one star fill the screen) but it loses the differentiation the user expects.

User intent: keep the foundation physical, use the existing star-exaggeration slider (K knob) to amplify differences for visualization purposes — not bake exaggeration into the formula itself.

# Proposed direction

1. **Drop or soften the brightness saturation** in the navigate-mode appSize formula. The √brightness PSF shape stays; the cap becomes a soft asymptote (`appSize = uSizeMax * (1 + log(1 + extraBrightness))` or similar) instead of a hard clamp. Bright stars genuinely differ in size again.

2. **Wire the exaggeration slider** so users who want stronger discrimination can amplify the spread without distorting the underlying physics. The slider modulates a power on the brightness term: `appSize = mix(uSizeMin, uSizeMax, brightness^(0.5 / K))` — K=1 is the physical case, K>1 exaggerates.

3. **Verify** by checking pairs across magnitude regimes:
   - Sol vs Barnard at 5e-3 pc (should be ~5×, currently identical)
   - Sirius A vs Sol from 10 pc (should be modest difference)
   - Rigel vs Vega from 100 pc (should be order-of-magnitude bigger)

# Out of scope (filed separately)

Spectral/temperature-aware perceived brightness — see stellata-a7d.2.10. V-band absmag is approximately photopic-weighted already, but cool/hot stars have small but real corrections.

# Files

- src/client/shaders/star.vert.glsl — appSize formula (~line 299)
- src/client/stellata.ts — uSizeMax, uSizeSpan, K knob plumbing
- docs/rendering.md — physical-fidelity claims would need updating

# Acceptance

- Sol vs Barnard at unfocused min distance show visibly different sizes, ratio in the right direction (Sol bigger).
- Existing chart-mode behaviour unchanged (chart-mode uses a separate code path).
- Star-exaggeration slider (K) modulates the spread monotonically and predictably.
- docs/rendering.md updated to describe the soft-saturation choice and reference the FAQ (stellata-doc-rendering).
close_reason: Soft-knee saturation implemented with uSizeKnee = 16 fixed (commit ee23ade / 9e9f5cf). Calibrated against Sol/Sirius/Barnard at 5e-3 pc and against the night-sky baseline; Alex confirmed the value feels right. Knee remains tunable in the debug panel for future re-calibration.

title: Unify auto-park distances to minDistForStar (focusStar default + warp source)
description:
User feedback during a7d.2 validation: "There seem to be lots of different distances that we use from stars, I would like to unify all of them so that there is a consistent experience for the user." Audit found the system is mostly already unified — most auto-park scenarios use minDistForStar(idx) (10% disc fill) — but several outliers remain.

# Existing distance-rules audit

| Scenario | Distance | Status |
|---|---|---|
| Warp arrival at destination | minDistForStar(dest) | ✓ |
| Observe→Navigate parking | minDistForStar(focal) | ✓ |
| Observe travel | minDistForStar(target) | ✓ |
| **Clean-slate app startup (no URL params)** | **hardcoded large default (≫ minDistForStar(Sol))** | ⚠ inconsistent |
| **Click-to-focus / search-select / URL restore** | **hardcoded 2 pc** | ⚠ inconsistent |
| **Warp source departure (pStart)** | **minDistForStar(DEST), not source — places camera INSIDE the source star when source is large (e.g. Betelgeuse)** | ⚠ inconsistent and wrong |
| Manual zoom floor | minOrbitDistForStar (90% disc fill) | DIFFERENT — and intentionally so |

# What to change

1. **Clean-slate startup**. App boot with no URL params currently parks the camera at a hardcoded distance from Sol that's much farther than minDistForStar(Sol). Investigate where the boot pose is set (likely in stellata.ts init or url-state.ts when no fragment is present) and replace the hardcoded distance with minDistForStar(solIdx) so first-load matches every other auto-park.

2. focusStar(starIndex, distancePc=2) at src/client/stellata.ts:1657. Default 2 pc → `this.minDistForStar(starIndex)`. Verify callers (search.ts:486, stellata.ts:2467, url-state.ts:692) still produce expected behavior. URL restore is the trickiest — if URL serialises a specific cam pose, focusStar might not even apply. Trace url-state.ts logic.

3. **startWarp pStart at stellata.ts:1770**. Currently `pStart = A + dirBack * endOffset` where endOffset = minDistForStar(destIdx). Should use minDistForStar(sourceIdx) for the source-side reorient point. **Failure mode this causes: warping FROM a giant star (Betelgeuse, Antares) to a small star (Sol) places the source-side reorient point INSIDE the giant** — minDistForStar(Sol) ≈ 5e-8 pc but Betelgeuse's radius is ≈ 4e-6 pc, so the camera starts the warp embedded inside Betelgeuse. The source-side park distance must scale with the SOURCE's radius.

   Concrete fix: pass sourceIdx through startWarp (or read from this.focusedStar at start and capture in warpState). Compute endOffsetSource = (state.destKind === 'star' && sourceIdx !== null) ? minDistForStar(sourceIdx) : endOffset. pStart = A + dirBack * endOffsetSource. pEnd unchanged (uses dest's endOffset).

# Why

Keeps the visual "park me at this star" experience consistent: every auto-arrival lands at 10% disc fill of the relevant target. Warp from Betelgeuse (huge R) to Sol (tiny R) shouldn't swoop close to Betelgeuse during the source-side reorient — that happens today because endOffset uses Sol's tiny park distance.

# Acceptance

- App boot lands at minDistForStar(Sol) — same as warping back to Sol from elsewhere.
- focusStar() callers (click, search, URL) park at minDistForStar(idx) — 10% disc fill of target — for both small and giant stars.
- Warp source-side reorient stays at sensible distance from the source (10% of source's disc fill) regardless of destination's size, so the camera is never embedded inside a giant source star.
- No regression for warp from Sol-class to Sol-class (where it didn't matter).
- Manual smoke: app boot, warp Betelgeuse→Sol, Sol→Betelgeuse, Sirius→Vega; observe→navigate park; search-select; click-to-focus.

# Branch

worktree-a7d.2-physical-disc-geometry, PR #22.
close_reason: Implemented and pushed in commit 1599624 on PR #22. Boot park, focusStar default, click-from-unfocused, and warp source-side reorient now all use minDistForStar(idx) (or cloudViewingDistancePc for cloud sources). Two control-side bugs surfaced and fixed during smoke: minDistance ordering inside focusStar (was clamping against old focal star's minOrbit) and the unfocus jump-out (minDistance bump pushed camera outward when sitting close). Click-from-unfocused on a star now calls focusStar instead of setOrbitTarget for unified UX. setOrbitTarget remains for URL-restore where cam/tgt are explicit. Discovered during smoke: unfocusing while close to a focal star disengages the uPinFocusToCenter pin (target.lengthSq() < 1e-12 invariant breaks when recenterOrigin shifts target to former focal star's world position), causing the star to jump on-screen. Also HUD ring disappears while Sol/GC arrows remain. Filed separately.

title: Fade Sol/GC/distance arrows on close approach (disc covers standard chevrons)
description:
After a7d.2's angular-diameter formula, on close approach to a focused star the rendered disc grows large enough to start covering the navigate-mode reference arrows that emanate from the focused-star anchor. The Sol arrow, GC arrow, and distance-vector chevron all attach at SHAFT_START_PX = 28 px (focus-ring rim + halo gap) and extend outward; once the disc crosses that radius the proximal end of every arrow disappears under the disc.

# Why

This is a UX problem in two directions:

1. Mid-zoom the partially-buried arrows look broken — half-shaft sticking out of the star disc.
2. At very close range distant context becomes irrelevant. Sol/GC are far-field references; the user is now in a near-field regime where future content (planets, exoplanets, binary orbits) is the relevant story. Hide the far-field chrome so the local view reads cleanly.

# What

Compute a single fade alpha per frame in navigate mode and apply it to **all three reference arrows** (Sol, GC, distance-vector). Trigger the fade on coverage of the **standard chevrons** (Sol/GC), not the distance-vector — the distance-vector can be very long, so basing the trigger on it would leave Sol/GC fully buried before fade engages.

# Fade rule

\`\`\`
shaftStartPx       = FOCUS_RING_RADIUS_PX + RING_HALO_GAP_PX = 28
solLenPx, gcLenPx  = current rendered shaft length of each
                     (= clamp(projAlong - shaftStart - targetMargin, 0, ARROW_PIXEL_LENGTH))
refLenPx           = max(solLenPx, gcLenPx)
discRadiusPx       = renderedSizePx(focusedIdx) / 2
coverage           = max(0, discRadiusPx - shaftStartPx) / refLenPx
fadeT              = smoothstep(0.5, 0.75, coverage)   // cubic ease (3t^2-2t^3)
alpha              = 1 - fadeT
\`\`\`

Use the **dynamic max** of the two standard arrows (not a constant 110) so that when one arrow is shortened by Sol/GC projecting close to the focused star, the fade engages earlier — visually consistent. If both are zero (Sol focused → Sol arrow hidden, GC clipped) treat alpha as 0.

# Scope

- **Navigate mode only.** Observe mode unaffected (camera is parked at the focal star, no focal-disc on screen). Function returns 1 outside navigate.
- **Rings stay as-is.** Focus ring's existing hide-when-disc>48 is correct UX (the ring identifies the star against the starfield; redundant when the star fills your view). HUD ring (observe-mode) and POI rings unchanged.
- **POI arrows (observe-mode) unchanged** — same reason as observe-mode HUD arrows: no focal-disc problem there.

# Acceptance

- Approach Betelgeuse / supergiant: as the disc grows, Sol/GC chevrons and any active distance-vector smoothly fade out together, reaching invisible before the disc fills the screen.
- Approach Sol-class star to orbit floor: same behaviour.
- Pull back: arrows fade back in at the same disc-radius thresholds.
- Sol/GC/distance-vector all share one alpha — never one fading while the others are full opacity.
- Observe mode: zero behavioural change.

# Files

- src/client/stellata.ts — new public method getNavigateArrowFadeAlpha(); fade calc; called from updateGalacticLayers before hudOverlay.update so both consumers see the same value within a frame.
- src/client/hud-overlay.ts — Sol + GC arrow path/bg/label opacity = alpha. Two-pass refactor in update() to compute both shaft lengths first, then derive alpha (or accept it as opts), then render.
- src/client/distance-vector-overlay.ts — dist-line/lineBg/label opacity = alpha (read via stellata.getNavigateArrowFadeAlpha()).
- src/client/focus-ring-overlay.ts — untouched.

# Branch

worktree-a7d.2-physical-disc-geometry, PR #22.
close_reason: Implemented in PR #22. Navigate-mode arrow fade gates on max(actually drawn sol shaft, actually drawn gc shaft) with smoothstep over coverage [0.5, 0.75]. View-space dir replaces aux-step for direction derivation, eliminating per-arrow snap when targets are behind the camera. Peak-amplitude disc keeps fade stable across variability cycle. Hold source-mode alpha through observe transition; snap on completion when disc hides. Debug HUD: debug.arrows().

title: Remaining centering issues after uPinFocusToCenter pin (warp arrival + other transitions)
description:
After commit 5035edf shipped the uPinFocusToCenter mechanism (force-pins focused star to NDC (0,0) bypassing float32 matrix cancellation in star.vert.glsl), the user confirmed Sol-on-default-load drift is fixed. But they report remaining centering problems on warp arrival and likely other camera transitions.

# Context

PR #22, branch worktree-a7d.2-physical-disc-geometry. Latest commit 5035edf "Force focused star to NDC (0,0); raise unfocused floor to 5e-3 pc".

The pin works as follows:
- Shader (src/client/shaders/star.vert.glsl): `if (gl_InstanceID == uPinFocusToCenter) centreClip = projectionMatrix * vec4(0.0, 0.0, -dPc, 1.0);` — replaces the matrix-multiply path with a single canonical projection.
- JS (src/client/stellata.ts:2702-2710): per-frame guard. Pin engages when (focusedStar !== null) && (cameraMode === 'navigate') && !warpState && !aimState && controls.target.lengthSq() < 1e-12.
- The target.lengthSq() check disengages the pin if the user pans the target away from the focused star's local origin.

Also raised GLOBAL_MIN_DIST_PC from 1e-9 → 5e-3 pc (line 170): unfocused zoom can no longer enter the float32-cancellation regime. To get closer than 5e-3 pc, focus a star (then per-star physical orbit floor applies).

# Why warp arrival might still drift

finishWarp() (stellata.ts:1841) sequence on navigate-mode arrival:
1. line 1873: camera.position = B - forward * endOffset (B in source-local frame; origin still on source A)
2. line 1874: camera.lookAt(B)
3. line 1876: controls.target = B (still in source-local frame, non-zero)
4. line 1877: warpState = null
5. line 1894: setFocus(destIdx) → recenterOrigin to dest. After recenter: camera at -forward*endOffset (local origin = dest), target = (0,0,0).

After step 5 returns, animate() resumes at line 2698 with the recentered state. uPinFocusToCenter update at lines 2702-2710 sees focusedStar=destIdx, target.lengthSq()=0, mode=navigate → pin engages on first post-warp frame.

So the pin SHOULD work for warp arrival. Either:
(a) my analysis is wrong somewhere
(b) pin engages but the visible drift has a different cause
(c) the centering issue is mid-warp (not arrival), and the user is interpreting it as arrival

Mid-warp: floating origin stays on source A through the animation. Destination B is at local position (B_world - A_world), not (0,0,0). Pin can't engage on B because focusedStar = source. The destination drifts naturally as the camera moves toward it; whether that reads as "drift" or "expected motion" depends on the visual expectation.

# Investigation steps

1. Reproduce. Warp from default view to several stars: Sol, Sirius A, Vega, Rigel, Betelgeuse. Note exactly when/where the off-center happens — last frame of warp? First frame post-arrival? Subtle vs gross?
2. Verify the pin is engaging post-arrival. Add a temporary console.log to the per-frame uPinFocusToCenter update, or check the uniform's value via debug.
3. Check if focusedStar matches gl_InstanceID for the rendered star (should be, since gl_InstanceID is the catalog index = star's row in instance buffer).
4. Other transitions to also check while at it:
   - aimAt (stellata.ts:2004) — animated rotation
   - focusStar() teleport (stellata.ts:1657) — instant jump
   - double-click teleport (stellata.ts:2528 dblClickAimPoint)
   - observe-mode entry / exit (observe-transition)
   - search-result selection (search.ts:486 → focusStar)
   - URL-state restore on load (url-state.ts:692)

# Possible additional pin variant

If mid-warp drift toward dest matters: extend the pin to ALSO engage on the destination during warp's final phase. Mechanism: pass uPinFocusToCenter = warpState.destIdx during warp's last ~30%, but with a different invariant — the destination is at controls.target which during warp = B in source-local frame ≠ origin. Would need a generalised pin: "this gl_InstanceID's vertex projects to whatever NDC the controls.target would project to," i.e. force its centreClip = projection * (cam-relative-target-position, 1). More complex; only attempt if simpler pin doesn't fix the report.

# Acceptance

- Destination star sits at viewport center on warp arrival (last animation frame and first post-arrival frame indistinguishable).
- focusStar/aimAt/double-click/search-select/URL-restore similarly center their target on first frame.
- Verified across stars of varying R: Sol (1 R_sun), Sirius A (1.7), Vega (2.4), Rigel (79), Betelgeuse (~887).
- No regression of the working Sol close-approach (commit 5035edf).

# Files

- src/client/stellata.ts:1841 finishWarp, :1735 startWarp, :2702 per-frame pin update, :2004 aimAt, :1657 focusStar
- src/client/shaders/star.vert.glsl (uPinFocusToCenter use site)
notes:
Root cause of warp-arrival drift identified and fixed in finishWarp() navigate-mode path.

# Diagnosis

finishWarp() wrote camera.position and controls.target in **source-local frame** using B = starLocalPosition(destIdx) — this reads from _localPositions, a Float32Array. setFocus(destIdx) was then called, whose recenterOrigin shifts target/camera by dx = catalog.positions[destIdx] - worldOffset, computed fresh in float64.

B (Float32-truncated) and dx (float64-fresh) are the 'same' displacement only to within Float32 ULP. After the shift:
- controls.target should be (0,0,0) in dest-local frame
- It's actually (B - dx) ≈ ±|AB|·1e-7 magnitude residual

For Sol→Rigel (|AB| ≈ 265 pc): residual ≈ 1-5×10⁻⁵ pc. Camera distance to Rigel = endOffset = minDistForStar(Rigel) ≈ 3.4×10⁻⁵ pc. **Residual is comparable to camera distance** → significant NDC offset (~30% of screen, matches user report).

Pin guard at stellata.ts:2702-2710 requires target.lengthSq() < 1e-12 to engage. Residual lengthSq ~1e-9 to 1e-8 → guard fails → pin disengages → dest renders at the actual (wrong) projected position.

For consecutive Rigel→Sol warp: endOffset = minDistForStar(Sol) ≈ 5×10⁻⁸ pc. Residual ≈ 5×10⁻⁵ pc → ratio ~1000× → Sol fully off-screen. Matches the second user report.

# Fix

In finishWarp's navigate-star branch, after setFocus(destIdx) recenters origin onto the destination, snap controls.target to (0,0,0) and camera.position to -forward·endOffset directly in the clean dest-local frame. No round-trip through the source-local→dest-local subtract → no float32 residual → pin engages correctly on first post-arrival frame.

# Other transitions audited

- focusStar (click/search/URL restore): same shape of bug, but endOffset = 2 pc default makes residual/distance ratio ~1e-7 — sub-pixel, not visually observable. Will become observable if/when stellata-a7d.2.4 unifies focusStar's default to minDistForStar(idx). At that point apply the same pattern.
- aimAt: no recenter, not affected.
- observe arrival: target placement in source-local frame too, but observe doesn't use trackball orbit so target offset doesn't affect rendering.

# Verification

- npm run typecheck: clean
- npm test: 246/246 pass
- Dev server: in browser, focus Sol (default), warp to Rigel — should land centred and stay centred at full zoom-in. Then warp back to Sol — Sol should be on-screen and centred.
close_reason: Fixed in commit d6c97da. Root cause: finishWarp wrote target/camera in source-local frame (B from Float32 _localPositions) then setFocus's recenterOrigin shifted by dx computed fresh in float64 — Float32 ULP mismatch left controls.target at a ~|AB|·1e-7 residual that disengaged the pin guard on long warps. Fix re-anchors target/camera in clean dest-local frame after setFocus runs. Verified by user: Sol→Rigel→Sol round-trip stays centred.

title: Investigate luminosity-class differentiation in star rendering (red dwarf vs red giant)
description:
Audit how well the current star renderer reflects the physics of different luminosity classes. Concrete observation: Betelgeuse (M1-2 Ia-ab red supergiant, ~10^5 Lsun, R ~ 700-1000 Rsun) and Barnard's Star (M4 V red dwarf, ~0.0035 Lsun, R ~ 0.2 Rsun) do not look as different as they should — a red supergiant and a red dwarf are ~7-8 orders of magnitude apart in luminosity and ~3-4 orders in radius, so they should read as visually distinct on screen. May be partly an apparent-magnitude artefact (Barnard's is 9.5 ly away, Betelgeuse is ~550 ly), but the suspicion is that the disc-radius / glow profile is collapsing the giants and dwarfs toward similar visual sizes once the camera is close to either.

What to investigate:
- How physicalRadius() in scripts/catalog-pure.ts handles luminosity class — does it actually pull dwarfs down and giants up, or does absmag dominate and class get washed out?
- The disc/glow super-Gaussian profile (docs/rendering.md): does the plateau-vs-Gaussian morph differentiate I/II/III from IV/V at typical camera distances, or does it converge?
- Spectral-class colour: are M dwarfs and M giants colour-coded differently? They have similar surface temperatures but very different radii — colour alone won't distinguish them.
- What known stars to A/B compare for ground truth: Betelgeuse vs Barnard's Star, Antares vs Proxima, Aldebaran vs 61 Cygni, Rigel (B8 Ia) vs Sirius (A1 V). Note their canonical L/R values and check what Stellata renders.

Decide whether the visual gap actually reflects physics or whether the renderer needs a luminosity-class-aware adjustment. This is exploratory — the outcome may be 'it's correct, the dynamic range just looks compressed' or 'we need a class-dependent size/brightness multiplier'.

title: Search/typeahead: keep DOM, only toggle .active class on arrow-nav
description:
Surfaced in code review (stellata-y84).

src/client/search.ts:94 and src/client/constellation-typeahead.ts:69 both use the same anti-pattern: every keystroke AND every ArrowUp/ArrowDown nav rebuilds the entire results `<ul>` via `resultsEl.innerHTML = ''` followed by per-result `appendChild`. Arrow-nav only changes which row has the `active` class; tearing down and rebuilding the whole list is unnecessary and noticeable as jank with the typeahead's 88-entry constellation list.

Fix: For arrow-nav, just toggle the `active` class on the previously-hovered and newly-hovered `<li>` in place. Rebuild only when the query changes.

Severity: HIGH (visible UX latency on arrow-nav).
notes: Fix shipped in PR #29 (branch worktree-eya-typeahead-arrow-nav, commit 8cbb926). Approach: added a setHover() helper in both src/client/search.ts (SearchBox method) and src/client/constellation-typeahead.ts (closure). Toggles .active on prev and new <li> in place; ArrowUp/ArrowDown call it instead of the full rebuild. renderResultsDom/renderDom still run on query change. Typecheck clean, 254/254 tests pass. Browser smoke pending review.

title: Star vertex shader: move dustExtinctionAV below visibility prefilter
description:
Surfaced in code review (stellata-y84).

src/client/shaders/star.vert.glsl:225 — `dustExtinctionAV` runs a 48-tap 3D-texture raymarch through the dust field for **every star instance every frame**, including stars that fail the visibility prefilter (`visible`/`magOk`/`distOk`/`spectOk`) further down the shader (line 244+). With 313k stars, this is the dominant vertex-shader cost.

Fix: Move the visibility prefilter above the dust raymarch. Compute the unextincted apparent magnitude first, fail-fast on stars that are out of band, then refine with extinction only for the survivors.

Caveat: stars near the magnitude threshold could pop in/out as extinction is applied/skipped. May need a small magnitude buffer (e.g. allow extra 0.5 mag headroom in the prefilter so dust-darkened stars within tolerance still get the raymarch).

Severity: HIGH (single biggest per-frame GPU win in the shader chunk).

title: Catalog binary: 0x10 flag bit semantics + inferBinaries pairing semantics
description:
Surfaced in code review (stellata-y84).

Two distinct issues in scripts/build-catalog.ts and scripts/catalog-pure.ts:

**A. 0x10 flag bit semantics diverge between two writers**
- `applyDoublesFlag` (build-catalog.ts:367) sets `0x10` on every star whose HIP appears in the CCDM doubles set — applies to both components.
- `inferBinaries` (catalog-pure.ts:287) sets `0x10` only on the brighter member of a geometric pair.

The renderer reads bit `0x10` with one assumed meaning, but receives a mix. Either align both writers to the same semantics (mark all components, or only primaries) or split into two bits.

**B. inferBinaries produces a directed nearest-neighbour graph, not mutual pairs**
- catalog-pure.ts:269-282 finds each star's nearest neighbour and assigns it as `companionIdx` without checking that the relationship is mutual. The header comment (line 226-229) implies pair semantics ("record as this star's companion and flag the brighter of the pair").
- Result: A→B and B→C is possible. The "primary" pick at line 287 is per-i and overlaps incoherently in 3-star clusters.

Fix: Decide between mutual-only pairs (post-process to require A→B ∧ B→A) or document the directed-graph behaviour explicitly. Either way, align the renderer's interpretation.

Severity: CRITICAL — the binary feeds every client.
notes: Fix landed in PR #13 (claude/fix-binary-flag-semantics). Both writers now treat 0x10 as 'primary anchor of a system, at most one per system': inferBinaries flags only the brighter member of mutual pairs (companionIdx remains directed for renderer use), applyDoublesFlag groups CCDM HIPs by CCDM_ID and picks brightest per group. KNOWN_VISUAL_DOUBLES restructured into {components, reason} systems so 61 Cyg A+B share one group. Tests updated; rebuild stats: 14 mutual geometric pairs + 12384 new CCDM primaries on top, 9-system overlap shows agreement between writers.

title: GPU resource disposal audit — Stellata.dispose() leaks materials/geometries
description:
Surfaced in code review (stellata-y84).

`Stellata.dispose()` (src/client/stellata.ts:2912-2914) currently disposes only a subset of GPU resources. The following are not released:

- `coreMaskMaterial` (separate from the three star materials)
- `particleMesh.geometry` and `particleMaterial`
- `clouds` (MolecularClouds layer)
- `galacticDisc`
- `galacticGrid`
- `hudOverlay` if it owns any GPU resources

In Three.js, every Material/Geometry/Texture/RenderTarget allocates GPU memory and must be `.dispose()`d explicitly. The current dispose() leaks all of the above on teardown. Today this is academic since dispose() runs only on page navigation, but it would matter for any future hot-reload / re-init path and for the in-flight tests around scene swapping.

Fix: Walk the dispose chain end-to-end and verify each layer/material is released. Easiest path: have each owned layer expose its own `dispose()` and call it from `Stellata.dispose()`.

Severity: CRITICAL (real leak, but currently low-impact).
close_reason: Fixed in PR #14 — dispose() now releases coreMaskMaterial, particleMesh+particleMaterial, clouds, galacticDisc, galacticGrid alongside the previously-disposed star geometry/materials and milkyway.

title: Thorough code review of production code for quality issues
description:
Conduct a comprehensive code review of all production code in src/ and scripts/ looking for:

- Inefficiencies (hot path waste, redundant work, suboptimal data structures)
- Bad style (inconsistent naming, unclear control flow, awkward abstractions)
- DRY violations (duplicated logic, repeated patterns that should be extracted)
- Dead code (unused exports, unreachable branches, stale flags)
- Premature abstractions or over-engineering
- Missing or misplaced comments (WHY comments needed; WHAT comments to remove)
- Unnecessary complexity (could be simpler without losing capability)
- Type-safety gaps or any-typed escape hatches
- Resource handling (event listener leaks, observer cleanup, GPU resource disposal)

Output: a triaged list of findings, each with file:line references and a recommended fix. Group by severity. File follow-up beads issues for non-trivial fixes; apply trivial cleanups directly.
notes:
Reviewed all 54 production source files (12,735 LOC) across src/ and scripts/. Triaged findings into 22 follow-up issues filed with `discovered-from:stellata-y84`.

**P1 (critical)** — 2 issues:
- stellata-c2l: GPU disposal audit (Stellata.dispose leaks)
- stellata-7is: 0x10 flag bit + inferBinaries pairing semantics

**P2 (high)** — 11 issues:
- Bugs: stellata-3s2, stellata-470, stellata-6oy, stellata-adr, stellata-mu9
- Perf: stellata-0a7 (shader dust prefilter), stellata-20w (pickStar), stellata-63f (Vector3 alloc), stellata-a0n (url-state hash), stellata-akk (SVG dirty-track), stellata-eya (typeahead arrow-nav)

**P3 (medium)** — 9 issues:
- DRY: stellata-snv, stellata-vjj, stellata-wk2, stellata-77m, stellata-v2j, stellata-075
- Cleanup: stellata-r48, stellata-t6l, stellata-nqb (rollup of small comment/WHY/dead-code findings)

**Trivial cleanups applied directly** (single commit):
- chart-labels.ts:240 — `let` → `const lastCentroidCamPos`
- chart-mode.ts:33 — `bindChartMode` return type `: void`
- molecular-clouds.ts:184 — `idx ?? null` simplify
- theme-toggle.ts — drop unused `getTheme()` + `currentMode` write-only state
- milkyway.ts:120 — collapse unused `GAL_TO_ICRS_M3` into one-line `ICRS_TO_GAL_M3` derivation

Typecheck clean, 244/244 tests pass.

**Overall impressions** from agents (synthesized):
- Hot-path discipline is generally strong (scratch quaternions, sortedDistFromSol prefilter for the core-mask gate, instanced star pipeline) but a recurring theme is per-frame Vector3 allocation across stellata.ts, the SVG overlays, and url-state. Adopting the `xxxInto(idx, out)` and dirty-tracked attribute write patterns from chart-labels.ts is the single biggest perf win available.
- Shader hygiene is good (calibration comments on every magic uniform); biggest single GPU win is moving the 48-tap `dustExtinctionAV` raymarch below the visibility prefilter.
- Type safety has one concrete hole (url-state FilterState patch) plus untyped CSV row + search-index entry in build-catalog.
- Binary format hygiene is solid but constants are duplicated across writer/verifier/reader; a shared layout map would prevent silent drift.
- DRY: clearest wins are modal dismissal (×3 modals), hud-overlay/poi-overlay arrow algorithm (~80 LOC duplicated), SearchBox vs constellation-typeahead (parallel implementations of the same UI), eight on*Handlers arrays in stellata.ts.

title: Replace disc formula, orbit floor, and parking distance with true angular geometry
description:
Replace the log-mapped disc formula with the physical-angular-diameter formula `θ = 2·atan(R · radiusFactor / d)`, and drive the manual-zoom orbit floor + warp parking distance from the same per-star physical radius.

Depends on Phase 0 (log depth buffer) — without it, Sol-class stars will z-fight on close approach.

# Geometry

```
θ_rad      = 2 · atan(R · radiusFactor / d)
physSizePx = θ_rad · viewport.y / fov_y_rad
```

Slots in at `star.vert.glsl:294` (current `physSize = sizeAtRef * (uRefDistPc / dPc) * radiusFactor`). Same swap also needed inside the variability headroom precompute (`star.vert.glsl:203-206`).

`pxSize = max(appSize, physSize)` continues to give the natural blob↔disc crossover. `vPhysRatio = clamp(physSize / pxSize)` continues to drive the disc-pass / glow-pass split.

# Manual zoom floor (90% of fill-the-viewport)

Replaces `minOrbitDistForStar()` (`stellata.ts:2306-2316`):

```
ZOOM_FLOOR_FRACTION = 0.9
fov_y = camera.fov · π/180
fov_x = 2 · atan(tan(fov_y/2) · w/h)
fov_minor = min(fov_x, fov_y)
d_min(idx) = R_idx / tan(ZOOM_FLOOR_FRACTION · fov_minor / 2)
```

Preserve the binary half-angle bump (`Math.max(d_min, sep × BINARY_MIN_DIST_FACTOR)`).

Recompute on:
- Focus change (already wired at `stellata.ts:1045` and `:1876`).
- FOV change — wire into `setCameraFov()` (`stellata.ts:1473`).
- Window resize.

# Warp / observe-exit parking distance

Replaces `minDistForStar()` (`stellata.ts:2329-2350`):

```
TARGET_PARK_FRACTION = 0.10
d_park(idx) = R_idx / tan(TARGET_PARK_FRACTION · fov_minor / 2)
```

Floor at `2 × d_min(idx)` so the orbit floor never exceeds the parking distance. Preserve binary half-angle bump.

# CPU mirror

Rewrite `renderedSizePx()` (`stellata.ts:2237-2297`) to mirror the new geometry exactly. Variability `radiusFactor` continues to multiply `R` before the `atan`. `appSize` (perceptual blob) path stays unchanged.

# Uniforms — remove

| Removed                       | Reason                                                        |
|-------------------------------|---------------------------------------------------------------|
| `uPhysMinPx` / `uPhysMaxPx`   | No more pixel-range mapping.                                  |
| `uLogRMin` / `uLogRMax`       | No more log-span normalisation.                               |
| `uRefDistPc`                  | No artificial reference distance.                             |
| `DEFAULT_MIN_DIST_PC = 0.005` | Replaced by per-star `d_min(R)`. Keep a small global default (e.g. `1e-9` pc) for the unfocused-around-origin orbit floor. |
| `TARGET_APPROACH_DISC_PX = 10`| Replaced by `TARGET_PARK_FRACTION = 0.10`.                    |

# Uniforms — add

- `uFovYRad` (float) — fed from `camera.fov · π/180`. Updated in the constructor and in `setCameraFov()`.
- (Alternative: derive from `uViewport` + a `uPxPerRad` scalar — `uFovYRad` is simpler.)

# Files

- `src/client/shaders/star.vert.glsl` — replace physSize formula at line ~294 and ~203-206; drop dead uniform declarations; add `uFovYRad`.
- `src/client/stellata.ts`:
  - Line 468 — Phase 0 already touched this.
  - Lines 483-488 — Phase 0 already touched this.
  - Line 501 — `controls.minDistance` initial value uses the new global default.
  - Lines 593-616 (shared uniforms) — drop the dead uniforms; add `uFovYRad`.
  - Lines 2227-2229 — delete `computePhysMaxPx()`.
  - Lines 2237-2297 — rewrite `renderedSizePx()`.
  - Lines 2306-2316 — rewrite `minOrbitDistForStar()`.
  - Lines 2329-2350 — rewrite `minDistForStar()`.
  - Constants block (lines 171-190): remove `DEFAULT_MIN_DIST_PC`, `TARGET_APPROACH_DISC_PX`; add `TARGET_PARK_FRACTION`, `ZOOM_FLOOR_FRACTION`, `GLOBAL_MIN_DIST_PC` (e.g. `1e-9`).
  - Line 246 export — `DEFAULT_MIN_DIST_PC` is exported; `grep -rn DEFAULT_MIN_DIST_PC src/` and migrate or drop.
  - `setCameraFov()` (line 1473) — push the new `fov_y_rad` into `uFovYRad`.
- `src/client/distance-vector-overlay.ts:100` — switch destination offset from `getFilter().sizeMax` to `stellata.renderedSizePx(destIdx)` so the chevron lands on the disc edge regardless of star size.
- Docs (commit alongside): `docs/rendering.md`, `docs/camera-modes.md`.

# Verification (manual smoke + tests)

1. **Background stars unchanged.** Default view, naked-eye preset. Sirius / Betelgeuse / Vega pixel-identical to current `main`.
2. **Resolved-disc ratio matches physics.** Warp to Betelgeuse and Sirius A in turn at the same FOV. On-screen disc ratio at equal `d/R` matches `R_Betel / R_Sirius`. (Old behaviour: identical 10 px discs.)
3. **Close approach to Sol-class star.** Focus a Sol-like star, zoom in fully. Disc grows until ~90% of viewport minor axis, then stops. No clipping, no z-fighting.
4. **Close approach to a white dwarf** (40 Eridani B, Sirius B). Same — fills ~90% of viewport at d_min.
5. **Close approach to Betelgeuse.** Disc fills ~90% of viewport at much greater absolute distance than Sirius A. Pulsation visibly grows/shrinks the radius.
6. **FOV change at zoom limit.** While zoomed to fill viewport, change FOV via `+`/`-`. Orbit floor recomputes; star tracks the new "fills 90% of viewport minor axis" target.
7. **Overlays follow the new disc.** Disc mask, focus ring, distance vector chevron all align with the rendered disc edge for both small and giant destination stars.
8. **No depth artefacts.** At d_min on Sol-class, distant stars (200 kpc) still render correctly behind the focused disc.
9. **Mechanical:** `npm run typecheck`; `npm test` (244 regression tests); smoke-test warp, observe mode, chart mode.

# Acceptance

All nine verification steps pass.

# Plan file

`~/.claude/plans/resume-planning-from-users-alexm-claude-reflective-wombat.md`
notes:
Implementation landed on branch worktree-a7d.2-physical-disc-geometry, PR #22. Mechanical gates pass (typecheck, 246 tests, build).

# Status as of 2026-05-03 (post-context-clear handoff)

Scope expanded mid-validation: a7d.2 itself shipped the geometry rewrite, but user-facing UX work surfaced several follow-ups. All filed as sub-issues:

- stellata-a7d.2.1 (P1, BUG) — Remaining centering issues post-pin (warp arrival flagged). NEXT WORK.

title: Physical-scale rewrite of star sizing & close approach
description:
Rewrite the star disc / close-approach pipeline so that resolved discs match true angular geometry (`θ = 2·atan(R/d)`) and the orbit + parking distances scale with each star's physical radius.

# Why

Stellata's stated intent is *physical reality with perceptual exaggeration only where the eye demands it.* Today the close-approach pipeline breaks that contract in three places:

1. **Disc-pass size formula is non-physical.** `physSize = sizeAtRef × (uRefDistPc / dPc) × radiusFactor`, where `sizeAtRef` is a *log-mapped pixel target* — the catalogue's full radius span (white-dwarf → hypergiant) is compressed into `[2 px, 50% of viewport]` at an arbitrary reference distance of `0.005 pc`. The `1/d` falloff is correct geometry, but it's applied to a perceptually-warped base — so the on-screen ratio between Sol and Betelgeuse does **not** match `R_Sol / R_Betelgeuse`.
2. **Reference distance is locked to `controls.minDistance`.** `uRefDistPc` and the orbit floor are the same number (`DEFAULT_MIN_DIST_PC = 0.005 pc`). Past that floor, growth saturates instead of continuing as physics demands.
3. **Auto-park targets a uniform 10 px disc.** `TARGET_APPROACH_DISC_PX = 10` collapses the physical-radius hierarchy at warp arrival: a white dwarf and Betelgeuse arrive identical-looking. UX-coherent, physics-incoherent.

Background-star calibration (point-source perceptual blob driven by arcsec-per-pixel × exaggeration K) is **correct** and must remain pixel-identical after this change. Only the resolved disc and the close-range floors change. Future work (exoplanets, solar-system mode) needs this physical foundation.

# Approach (summary)

For a star of physical radius `R` (pc) at camera distance `d` (pc):

```
θ_rad      = 2 · atan(R · radiusFactor / d)        (true angular diameter)
physSizePx = θ_rad · viewport.y / fov_y_rad        (pixel diameter, screen-aligned quad)
```

`pxSize = max(appSize, physSize)` continues to give the natural crossover between the perceptual blob and the physical disc. Variable pulsation multiplies `R` (`R_eff = R · radiusFactor`). The `vPhysRatio` disc-pass / glow-pass split is now driven by *real* angular size.

Closest manual zoom (90% of fill-the-viewport):
```
ZOOM_FLOOR_FRACTION = 0.9
fov_minor = min(fov_x, fov_y)
d_min(star) = R / tan(ZOOM_FLOOR_FRACTION · fov_minor / 2)
```

Auto-park (warp arrival, observe-exit):
```
TARGET_PARK_FRACTION = 0.10
d_park(star) = R / tan(TARGET_PARK_FRACTION · fov_minor / 2)
```

Z-buffer precision is the critical enabler: at d_min for Sol-class stars (`R ≈ 2.25e-8 pc`, `d_min ≈ 5e-8 pc`), standard depth precision collapses. Phase 0 enables `logarithmicDepthBuffer: true` on the renderer and adds `<logdepthbuf>` chunks to every custom GLSL3 ShaderMaterial.

# Phasing

Tracked as two child tasks:
- **Phase 0** — Enable log depth buffer + add `<logdepthbuf>` chunks to all shaders. Pure prerequisite, no visual change expected.
- **Phase 1** — Replace disc formula, orbit floor, parking distance. Depends on Phase 0.

# Out of scope

- Star colour / luminosity-class softness (untouched).
- Background-star calibration (untouched — confirmed correct).
- Chart mode disc sizing (`uMonochrome > 0.5` path is independent).
- `star-tuning.ts` debug panel (its sliders don't expose the removed uniforms).

# Acceptance

1. Background stars pixel-identical to current `main` from default view (naked-eye preset).
2. Resolved-disc ratio between Betelgeuse and Sirius A at equal `d/R` matches `R_Betel / R_Sirius`.
3. Manual zoom to a focused star grows the disc until ~90% of viewport minor axis, then stops. Works for white dwarfs, Sol-class, and supergiants.
4. Warp arrival: every star arrives at 10% of viewport minor axis. Parking distance scales with R.
5. FOV `+`/`-` while at zoom limit recomputes the orbit floor live.
6. Disc mask, focus ring, distance-vector chevron all track the new disc size correctly.
7. No z-fighting at d_min for Sol-class stars; no banding artefacts in the milky-way / cloud / dust-particle layers.
8. `npm run typecheck` clean; `npm test` (244 regression tests) green.

# Plan file

Full plan at `~/.claude/plans/resume-planning-from-users-alexm-claude-reflective-wombat.md`.

title: Rebase PR #30 onto PR #22 after #22 lands — surgery plan
description:
PR #30 (epic 9mm cleanup batch 1) is open with 43 beads of work but cannot merge until PR #22 (stellata-a7d.2 physical disc geometry) lands first. PR #22 is the priority and supersedes ~5 of PR #30s refactors. This bead captures the rebase plan so the surgery is mechanical when the time comes.

# 3-way merge surface

Mechanical conflicts: 4 files, 6 hunks total.
- package.json — version bump (both bumped from 1.0.12; pick whichever is later)
- docs/camera-modes.md — both edited the camera.near prose; PR #22s wording about GLOBAL_MIN_DIST_PC supersedes
- src/client/hud-overlay.ts — constructor signature changed in #30 (Sol/GC click callbacks); cascade rewritten in #22
- src/client/url-state.ts — #30 added single dirty flag + OBSERVE_CAM_LOCAL; #22 added worldOffset field

Files that auto-merge cleanly: stellata.ts, arrow-path.ts, distance-vector-overlay.ts, shaders/star.vert.glsl, url-state.test.ts. Pleasant surprise — #22s edits live in the star-sizing math; #30s edits live in dispose/constructor/helper extraction.

# Semantically superseded beads (REOPEN these after rebase)

These #30 changes go against the grain of #22 and should be reverted during the rebase, with the matching bead reopened so a future session can address them under #22s new design:

- **9mm.3 (3-tier cascade)** — PR #22 redesigned to 2 tiers (target-projection + view-space, dropping the aux-step tier) with per-axis FOV scaling. screenDirFromCascade is no longer the right shape. Reopen 9mm.3 with note: cascade should follow #22s 2-tier design.

- **9mm.5 (projectToScreen hoist)** — PR #22 keeps projectToScreen local to hud-overlay.ts. The hoist to arrow-path.ts works against #22. Either drop the hoist (revert) or reopen 9mm.5 with note that consolidation should follow #22s structure.

- **9mm.46 (OVERLAY_NEAR_CLIP_PC = 1e-3)** — PR #22 changes the orbit floor to GLOBAL_MIN_DIST_PC = 1e-9 and overlays still read camera.near. The decoupling is moot. Revert and reopen 9mm.46 with note: re-evaluate after #22 lands; may no longer be needed.

- **9mm.20 / 9mm.24 (OBSERVE_CAM_LOCAL)** — PR #22 adds a worldOffset URL field and reframes the cam-default check entirely: when the frame isnt Sol-anchored, cam is always emitted regardless of value. The named constant lives inside a logic that #22 changes. Revert and reopen with note: re-evaluate the magic [0,0,0] under #22s worldOffset frame.

- **9mm.30 (single dirty flag)** — PR #22 adds another condition (`view.worldOffset`) to the controls.update OR. The dirty flag refactor still works but needs the new branch folded in. Resolve the conflict by adding `controlsDirty = true` to the worldOffset branch in #22s decoder. Bead can be closed without reopening.

# Beads that survive rebase intact

The other ~38 beads are independent of #22:
- All P1 dispose-chain fixes (9mm.29, 9mm.38, 9mm.40, 9mm.42)
- All catalog flag work (9mm.21, 9mm.25, 9mm.28, 9mm.32, 9mm.37) — touches scripts/catalog-pure.ts + scripts/build-catalog.ts which #22 doesnt touch
- All test additions
- Small cleanups: 9mm.2, 9mm.4, 9mm.8, 9mm.10, 9mm.13, 9mm.16, 9mm.18, 9mm.19, 9mm.23, 9mm.26, 9mm.31, 9mm.33, 9mm.35, 9mm.36, 9mm.41, 9mm.47, 9mm.48
- Doc updates: 9mm.22, 9mm.45, 9mm.50 (need conflict resolution against #22s doc edits but the additions themselves are orthogonal)

# Procedure when #22 lands

1. From the worktree:
   ```
   git fetch origin main
   git rebase origin/main
   ```
2. Conflict resolution per the four files above. For the superseded beads, prefer reverting #30s changes (i.e. keep #22s side) so the surgery is just `git checkout --theirs <file>` for the parts under #22s ownership, then re-apply only the #30 changes that arent superseded.
3. Reopen the 4 superseded beads (9mm.3, 9mm.5, 9mm.46, 9mm.20/24) via `bd update --status=open` and add a note like "Reopened after PR #30 rebase onto PR #22; the original direction is superseded by #22s [specific change]; re-evaluate the new starting point."
4. Bump package.json to whatever 1.0.X is next after #22.
5. Verify `npm run typecheck` and `npm test`.
6. Force-push the rebased branch.
7. Re-run the manual-smoke checklist on the rebased PR — especially the highest-priority items (HudOverlay click rewiring, camera roll-snap on both exit paths, build-catalog log lines).

# Why this strategy (vs the alternatives)

- *Merge #30 first*: would force the user to rebase the bigger, more important #22 — and several of #30s helpers are about to be obsoleted anyway.
- *Pull #22 into #30*: bundles the diffs and defeats the goal of reviewing #22 independently. Worst option.
- *Cherry-pick safe parts of #30 now*: tempting but cleaner done as a focused rebase after #22 lands rather than splitting #30 prematurely.

The rebase is meaningful but tractable — clear deletions of obsolete pieces, not knotty hunk-by-hunk reconciliation. PR #30s bug fix (camera roll-snap on observe hard-switch / focus-change) is the only user-visible change and its against an internal side-project, so the delay isnt urgent.

title: No precision regression test for log-depth ordering across the new dynamic range
description:
PR #7 expanded the camera frustum from `(near=1e-3, far=2e5)` to `(near=1e-10, far=2e5)` — a dynamic range of 15 orders of magnitude — and is relying on `logarithmicDepthBuffer: true` to keep front-back ordering correct across that range. There is no automated test that exercises this.

`npm test` (244 tests) has nothing depth-related: search.test, controls.test, distance-util.test, etc. all run pure JS. The PR's verification was visual ('default view renders identically to main') which catches gross regressions but won't catch precision drift at the extremes — a star at d ≈ 5e-8 pc (the a7d.2 target d_min) sorting incorrectly against a 200 kpc galactic-disc fragment, for example.

Why it matters: this is a prerequisite PR for stellata-a7d.2, where d_min drops to ~5e-8 pc. The whole point of enabling log-depth was to keep z-fighting away from the close-range disc rendering. A future change that re-disables log-depth, accidentally drops one of the eight chunk includes, or restores a per-shader `gl_FragDepth = gl_FragCoord.z` write would silently regress that — and no CI signal would catch it. Visual verification in dev mode is the only line of defence.

Suggested direction: a vitest test using a headless WebGL2 context (or three.js's existing test harness) that:
1. Renders two unit quads at known distances spanning [1e-8, 2e5] pc.
2. Reads back depth via a render target with `THREE.DepthTexture`.
3. Asserts pairwise depth ordering across at least 4-5 distance buckets.

If a full GL test is too heavy, a JS-side approximation: assert that for the chosen near/far/log-depth-buffer settings, the float32 `gl_FragCoord.z` produced by the matrix chain is monotonic across a sampled distance grid. Cheaper but only catches CPU-side regressions (e.g. someone resetting near to 1e-3).
notes: Deferred from PR #30 (2026-05-06 batch). Log-depth precision is a GPU concern — verifying it requires either rendering across the actual dynamic range (1e-10 to 3e4 pc) and reading depth-buffer samples, or a unit test of the log-depth chunk's varying math. The chunk is three.js stock code so it's not the unit being tested; what's worth pinning is end-to-end z-order at extreme distances. Pick up alongside 9mm.44 since both want a real WebGL2 renderer in tests. The depth-encoding section added to docs/rendering.md (this batch) documents the invariant in prose.

title: Off-screen sentinel early-returns skip <logdepthbuf_vertex> — undocumented load-bearing invariant
description:
Three vertex shaders early-return with a fixed off-screen `gl_Position` BEFORE the trailing `#include <logdepthbuf_vertex>`:

- src/client/shaders/star.vert.glsl:168 (uHideFocusIdx suppression) and :248 (visibility filter) — both `gl_Position = vec4(2.0, 2.0, 2.0, 1.0); … return;` ahead of the chunk include at line 320.
- src/client/shaders/dust-particle.vert.glsl:43 — `gl_Position = vec4(2.0, 2.0, 2.0, 1.0);` then `return;` at line 45 ahead of the chunk include at line 69.

The chunk's job is to populate the `vFragDepth` varying that the matching frag shader's `<logdepthbuf_fragment>` reads. Skipping the chunk leaves `vFragDepth` undefined for that vertex.

Why this is currently safe: every vertex of the instanced quad goes through the same code path (the early-return condition reads only per-instance attributes, not per-vertex), so all 4 verts of the quad land at the same off-screen NDC and the entire primitive is clipped before rasterization. The fragment shader never executes for that quad, so undefined `vFragDepth` doesn't leak into a depth write.

Why it's worth flagging: the safety is a nontrivial invariant that depends on (a) the off-screen sentinel being applied to ALL verts of the primitive, (b) NDC clipping discarding the whole primitive. A future change — say, a per-vertex variation in the off-screen position, or a switch to non-clipping primitive types — could let one quad have valid `vFragDepth` on three verts and undefined on the fourth, producing interpolated garbage and a stray near-z stamp. Nothing in the shaders documents this.

Suggested direction: cheapest fix is a one-line comment near each early-return ('// All 4 quad verts share this path; chunk skip is safe because the primitive is fully off-screen'). More defensive: include the chunk before each early-return so `vFragDepth` is always written. The chunk is cheap and idempotent.

title: Overlay near-plane clip threshold piggybacks on camera.near — semantically overloaded after log-depth switch
description:
Seven overlays read `camera.near` directly as the 'behind-camera / on-near-plane' clip threshold for screen-space projection:

- src/client/distance-vector-overlay.ts:163 — `const threshold = -camera.near;` (used twice for source + destination clipping)
- src/client/focus-ring-overlay.ts:66 — `if (v.z > -camera.near) ...`
- src/client/poi-overlay.ts:405 — `if (v.z >= -camera.near) return null;`
- src/client/disc-mask.ts:53 — `if (v.z > -camera.near) return false;`
- src/client/hud-overlay.ts:412 — `if (v.z >= -camera.near) return null;`
- src/client/constellation-overlay.ts:58 — `if (v.z > -camera.near) return null;`
- src/client/chart-labels.ts:748 — `if (projVec.z >= -camera.near) return null;`

PR #7 dropped `camera.near` from `0.001` pc (~3 mm view-space) to `1e-10` pc to give logarithmic depth buffer headroom for stellata-a7d.2's per-star physical sizing. That collapses the overlay clip threshold to effectively zero — overlays now keep rendering for objects in view-space `z ∈ (-1e-10, 0)`, which previously got clipped at `-0.001`. `camera.near` is now serving two unrelated purposes: GPU log-depth precision floor, and CPU overlay near-plane clipping. The two concepts no longer share a sensible value.

Why it matters: at the current orbit floor (DEFAULT_MIN_DIST_PC = 5e-3 pc) practical behavior is unchanged because the focused star's view-z (~-5e-3) clears both thresholds. But the implicit coupling is fragile — distance-vector-overlay.ts:178 also sets `endView.z = threshold - 1e-4` to nudge the clipped vertex behind the near plane; with threshold ≈ 0 that nudge becomes `z ≈ -1e-4` (in view-space pc), which differs from the prior `z ≈ -1.001e-3` by a factor of 10.

Suggested direction: introduce a named constant — e.g. `OVERLAY_NEAR_CLIP_PC = 1e-3` — exported from a shared module, and replace the seven `camera.near` reads with it. Decouples overlay clipping from log-depth precision. The PR description should ideally have called this out as a touched-but-not-tested interaction.

title: Add a smoke test for Stellata.dispose() — instantiate, dispose, instantiate again, no errors
description:
The c2l audit landed without any test coverage at the dispose() entrypoint, so the next time a new layer is added (and somebody forgets to plug it into dispose()) the regression has to be caught by manual review again — same path that left coreMaskMaterial / particleMesh / clouds / galactic{Disc,Grid} unreleased before this PR.

Leak detection per se is hard to unit-test (no easy way to assert a Three.js Material was disposed without monkey-patching), but the cheap, useful guard is a smoke test:

1. Instantiate Stellata with a tiny synthetic catalog (the test suite already builds these — see catalog-loader.test.ts).
2. Call dispose().
3. Instantiate a second Stellata against the same canvas / catalog.
4. Call dispose() again.
5. Assert no throws and (optionally) that disposed === true.

A tighter version: spy on each owned resource's dispose() via vi.spyOn before teardown, assert each was called exactly once. That catches future regressions where a new layer ships without dispose() integration.

Direction: new src/client/stellata.dispose.test.ts. Reuse the in-memory catalog fixture from catalog-loader.test.ts. Skip if WebGL isn't available in the vitest environment (jsdom may need a stub renderer).
notes: Deferred from PR #30 (2026-05-06 batch). Stellata's constructor instantiates THREE.WebGLRenderer which needs a real WebGL2 context. Running this in vitest requires either (a) headless-gl or a similar GL polyfill + jsdom, or (b) a Stellata test seam that lets a lightweight fake renderer flow through. Both are bigger than a unit test. Note that PR #30 already verified each owner's dispose() in isolation: DustField.dispose (9mm.38), HudOverlay.dispose (9mm.42), molecular-clouds.test.ts construction, MolecularClouds smoke. The remaining gap is the *integration* — does Stellata.dispose() walk all owners. Pick up when adding the first integration-test infrastructure.

title: No regression-prevention tests for the 5 bugs in PR #23
description:
PR #23 fixed 5 bugs (stellata-3s2/6oy/mu9/adr/a8w) with zero new vitest coverage. Each is a regression candidate; the test suite is exactly where these should land so future refactors don't silently undo the fix. Per-bug:

- **stellata-3s2** (unit-toggle): closure-captured buttons + idempotent bindUnitToggle. JSDOM test that calls bindUnitToggle twice on the same host and asserts only the second-bind's listeners fire is straightforward.
- **stellata-6oy** (FilterState patch type-safety): purely compile-time so a runtime test is hard, but a small `tsd`-style assertion or a test that constructs a Partial<FilterState> patch with a bogus key and expects ts2353 would pin the contract.
- **stellata-mu9** (setIsobar uniform rebind + blending restore): construct MolecularClouds with a stubbed catalog, call setIsobar(true, u1) → setIsobar(true, u1) → setIsobar(false, u1), and assert blending restores to AdditiveBlending and uMaxAppMag is === u1 throughout. No GL needed.
- **stellata-adr** (particle byte-length validation): unit test on a pure helper would require extracting the validation; alternatively a parser test that feeds a truncated ArrayBuffer to loadDustParticles (mocking fetch) and expects null.
- **stellata-a8w** (camera.up alignment): assert that after `camera.up.set(0,1,0).applyQuaternion(q)`, camera.up equals the camera's local +Y basis vector (a column of the rotation matrix). Pure linear algebra, no Three scene needed.

Why it matters: 5 bugs were caught by code review (stellata-y84) rather than by tests. Closing that loop is the difference between 'we found these once' and 'they're guarded'.

title: applyDoublesFlag has zero test coverage despite being a core writer of the same bit
description:
catalog-pure.test.ts has solid coverage for `inferBinaries` (including the new mutual-pair regression cases added in PR #13). But `applyDoublesFlag` and `parseHipCcdm` (build-catalog.ts:310-430) have NO unit tests — they live in build-catalog.ts which has no test file, while everything testable was deliberately moved to catalog-pure.ts.

The whole point of stellata-7is was that two writers diverged in their semantics. Half of that surface (the CCDM half) is still untested. A future contributor could rewrite `applyDoublesFlag` to flag every component of a CCDM group, and the only signal would be the rebuild log + visual chart-mode review. The semantic contract — "exactly one primary per CCDM group, picked by brightest absmag, idempotent with inferBinaries' bit" — needs pinning down.

Direction: move the pure parts of `parseHipCcdm` (TSV row parsing, MultFlag gate, group aggregation) and `applyDoublesFlag` (group → primary index) into catalog-pure.ts so they can be unit-tested. Then add tests covering: (1) brightest-of-group is the only one flagged, (2) groups with no catalog components are silently dropped, (3) override systems behave like file systems, (4) idempotency with a pre-existing 0x10 bit set by inferBinaries. The combined sibling 'unify behind markPrimary helper' refactor would naturally land most of this in catalog-pure too.

title: setIsobar / setMonochrome blending state is duplicated and uncoordinated (stellata-mu9)
description:
PR #23's setIsobar fix (molecular-clouds.ts:123-142) restores blending on toggle-off using the inline expression `this.mono ? NormalBlending : AdditiveBlending` — copy-pasted from setMonochrome's blending logic at line 110.

Two issues:

1. **DRY**: 'what blending mode should this material be in?' now lives in two places. Future changes to the per-mode blending (e.g. premultiplied-alpha tweak, new chart palette) have to be applied in both setMonochrome and setIsobar's else-branch in lockstep.

2. **State coordination gap**: setMonochrome unconditionally writes blending without consulting an active isobar, and setIsobar's toggle-off ignores nothing about the live monochrome state at the moment of the call (it does read this.mono, but only for the off-branch). If isobar is on and setMonochrome is called (e.g. theme change while chart-mode is active), the cloud blending will get clobbered to whatever setMonochrome decides regardless of isobar being live.

Suggested direction: Add a private `applyBlending()` that derives blending from (this.mono, this.isobar) — store `isobar` on the instance like `mono` — and have both setters mutate their flag and call applyBlending(). Single source of truth, no coordination race.

Note: the cloud overlay is shelved for v1; both bugs are latent. Worth doing before the layer is re-enabled.

title: Geometric and CCDM passes can double-flag stars in the same physical system
description:
PR #13 says the contract is "each binary system gets exactly one chart-mode wings glyph." But the two passes run independently and there's no consolidation:

1. `inferBinaries` (build-catalog.ts:676) flags the brighter of mutual geometric pairs.
2. `applyDoublesFlag` (build-catalog.ts:703) then flags the brightest catalog component of each CCDM group.

Both pick "brightest of group" but their groups are defined differently (3D nearest-neighbour mutual pairs vs. CCDM_ID grouping). They can disagree on which star is the anchor of the same physical system. Concrete failure mode:

- α Cen A and α Cen B are a mutual geometric pair → `inferBinaries` flags A (brighter).
